title: Macroscopic photon counting beating the Poisson noise limit
authors: Timon Schapeler, Fabian Schlue, Isabell Mischke, Michael Stefszky, Benjamin Brecht, Christine Silberhorn, Tim J. Bartley
abstract: Photon counting is a cornerstone of quantum optics. Here, we demonstrate precisely counting from 0 to over 9000 photons, beating the Poisson noise limit by at least $4.1~\mathrm{dB}$ across this range. We achieve sub-single-photon precision up to 276 photons per pulse. To do so, we multiplex eight intrinsically photon-number-resolving superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors across 128 temporal modes. We use a model-informed characterization of each of the 1024 detection bins, for optimal precision. We perform quantum detector tomography to reconstruct the positive operator valued measures (POVMs) of the complete device, which consists of $1.38\cdot10^8$ matrix elements. At the repetition rate of our experiment of $80~\mathrm{kHz}$, we can precisely count photons corresponding to an optical power of approximately $71~\mathrm{pW}$, bridging the gap from single-photon measurements to high-sensitivity optical power meters. A photon-number-resolving detector of this size, and the tools used to analyze it, will become increasingly important to characterize large quantum states, as well as tasks in precision metrology and optical power standards.
summary: The researchers developed a method to count large numbers of photons (up to 9000) with precision that exceeds the standard Poisson noise limit. By multiplexing superconducting nanowire detectors, they created a device that functions as a high-sensitivity optical power meter, useful for both quantum state characterization and precision metrology.
main_problem: The challenge of achieving precise photon-number resolution for high photon fluxes, specifically overcoming the Poisson noise limit in macroscopic photon counting.
main_result: Demonstration of precise photon counting from 0 to over 9000 photons, beating the Poisson noise limit by at least 4.1 dB and achieving sub-single-photon precision up to 276 photons per pulse.
method: Multiplexing eight superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors across 128 temporal modes and using model-informed characterization of 1024 detection bins alongside quantum detector tomography.
model_or_system: An experimental setup consisting of eight intrinsically photon-number-resolving superconducting nanowire single-photon detectors (SNSPDs) multiplexed into 1024 temporal detection bins.
key_observables: Photon number counts, Poisson noise limit, and Positive Operator Valued Measures (POVMs).
important_parameters: Photon range (0 to 9000+), noise reduction (4.1 dB), repetition rate (80 kHz), and optical power (71 pW).
main_assumptions: The accuracy relies on the model-informed characterization of the 1024 detection bins.
paper_structure: The paper presents an experimental demonstration of a high-capacity photon-counting device, detailing the multiplexing architecture, the characterization of detection bins, the reconstruction of POVMs via tomography, and the performance metrics regarding noise reduction and power sensitivity.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for quantum optics and precision metrology as it bridges the gap between single-photon detection and high-sensitivity optical power meters, providing a tool for characterizing large quantum states.
